title: Adlytic Meta Ads Knowledge Base
description: Production-grade advertising intelligence reference. Every metric carries definition, formula, benchmarks, thresholds, causes, recommendations, and edge cases. Used by Adlytic AI to ground recommendations in verified industry standards rather than general model knowledge.
currency_note: Benchmark monetary values are stated in their source currency (GBP £, USD $, EUR €). Adlytic stores money in minor units; IQD has factor 1 (no subunit). When comparing a stored value against a benchmark, normalise both to the same currency/major-unit basis before evaluating thresholds. Benchmarks are INDUSTRY AVERAGES, not official Meta guarantees.

threshold_semantics.healthy: Metric is within the desirable range; no action needed.
threshold_semantics.warning: Metric has drifted from healthy; investigate and prepare a corrective action.
threshold_semantics.critical: Metric breached an unacceptable bound; act now (pause, rotate creative, or restructure).
threshold_semantics.direction: Each metric declares whether HIGHER or LOWER is better via `better_when`. Thresholds are written in the natural reading direction for that metric.

sources.name: Motion Meta Ads Glossary
sources.use: Detailed explanations, practical usage, metric relationships.

sources.name: Pengwing Meta Ads Benchmarks 2026
sources.use: Industry-average benchmark ranges (not official Meta standards).

CTR.name: Click-Through Rate
CTR.definition: The percentage of impressions that resulted in a click on the ad. Meta's official definition: number of clicks divided by impressions.
CTR.meaning: Primary early signal of creative relevance and audience-message fit. A healthy CTR means the creative and hook are stopping the scroll and earning attention.
CTR.warning: Below 0.9% on a cold prospecting audience, or a sustained downward trend over 3-5 days.
CTR.critical: Below 0.7%, or a >30% drop from the campaign's own baseline (signals creative fatigue).
possible_causes: Weak or generic creative hook (first 3 seconds fail to stop scroll)
Audience-message mismatch or overly broad targeting
Creative fatigue from high frequency on the same audience
Wrong placement mix (e.g. forcing feed creative into Reels)
recommendations: Refresh the creative hook; test new opening 3 seconds
Tighten or re-segment the audience for closer message fit
Rotate in new creative variations if frequency is climbing
Split placements and let strong placements dominate
Strengthen the CTA and value proposition
best_practices: Distinguish link CTR (outbound) from all-CTR; optimise on the one tied to your objective
Read CTR alongside CVR — high CTR + low CVR points downstream, not at the creative
Use CTR as the earliest creative-quality read before enough conversions accumulate
common_mistakes: Judging CTR in isolation without conversion context
Comparing all-CTR to a link-CTR benchmark (apples to oranges)
Killing a creative on one low day instead of a trend
notes_edge_cases: Retargeting audiences naturally show higher CTR than cold prospecting — benchmark within audience type.
Very high CTR with poor CVR can indicate clickbait creative misaligned with the landing page.

link_CTR.name: Link Click-Through Rate (Outbound)
link_CTR.definition: Percentage of impressions that produced a click on a link leading off the ad (to a website or destination), as opposed to any click including reactions, comments, or media expansion.
link_CTR.meaning: A cleaner intent signal than all-CTR because it counts only clicks that move a user toward the destination.
link_CTR.warning: Gap between all-CTR and link-CTR widening (engagement clicks rising but link clicks flat).
link_CTR.critical: Link CTR below 0.4% on a conversion campaign.
possible_causes: Creative earns engagement but not destination intent
CTA does not communicate where the click leads
Slow or broken destination deterring clicks
recommendations: Make the destination value explicit in copy and CTA
Test a more direct CTA button
Verify landing page loads quickly on mobile
best_practices: Optimise conversion campaigns on link CTR, not all-CTR
common_mistakes: Reporting all-CTR as if it were link CTR
notes_edge_cases: On messaging objectives the analogous signal is the click-to-message rate, not link CTR.

CPC.name: Cost Per Click
CPC.definition: The average amount paid each time a user clicks the ad. Meta: advertiser payment amount per click on an ad unit.
CPC.meaning: Efficiency of buying attention. CPC is a downstream product of CPM and CTR: CPC = CPM / (CTR * 10).
CPC.warning: CPC trending above £1.80 (broad) without a CVR or AOV advantage to justify it.
CPC.critical: CPC above £4.00 on a cold audience with no conversion lift.
possible_causes: Low CTR inflating cost per click
High CPM from auction competition or narrow audience
recommendations: Improve CTR (better hook) — the fastest lever on CPC
Broaden audience to reduce CPM pressure
Refresh creative to lift relevance
Test new placements with cheaper inventory
best_practices: Treat CPC as derived: fix CTR or CPM rather than CPC directly
Compare CPC within the same audience temperature
common_mistakes: Optimising CPC at the expense of conversion quality
Ignoring that a higher CPC can still be profitable if CVR/AOV are high
notes_edge_cases: A rising CPC driven by rising CPM (not falling CTR) reflects market competition and may not be fixable via creative.

CPM.name: Cost Per Mille (Cost Per 1,000 Impressions)
CPM.definition: The cost to serve 1,000 impressions. 'Mille' is Latin for thousand (not million). Good Morning: cost to reach 1,000 people with one impression.
CPM.meaning: Auction-cost signal reflecting competition, audience quality, seasonality, and ad relevance. Rising CPM alone is not actionable — read it with CTR and CVR.
CPM.warning: CPM up >20% week-over-week without a matching audience or seasonal explanation.
CPM.critical: CPM above £35 on a broad audience, eroding ROAS with no quality offset.
possible_causes: Low ad relevance/quality ranking raising delivery cost
recommendations: Broaden audience to access cheaper inventory
Improve relevance (creative quality, engagement) to lower delivery cost
Diversify placements (Advantage+ placements)
Avoid over-narrow targeting that starves delivery
best_practices: Never act on CPM alone — pair with CTR/CVR
Expect and plan for seasonal CPM inflation
common_mistakes: Panicking over CPM rises that are pure seasonality
Chasing the lowest CPM at the cost of audience quality
notes_edge_cases: eCPM (effective CPM) is the publisher-side equivalent: estimated revenue / impressions * 1000.

CPA.name: Cost Per Acquisition / Cost Per Action
CPA.definition: The cost to generate one conversion (purchase, lead, install, or the campaign's optimisation event). Meta CPA: a model where the advertiser pays per defined action.
CPA.meaning: The core profitability gate. CPA must sit below the maximum sustainable acquisition cost implied by AOV and margin (or LTV).

benchmark.note: Highly objective- and industry-specific; benchmark against your own max sustainable CPA = AOV * margin (or LTV * target payback).

CPA.warning: CPA rising toward your break-even acquisition cost.
CPA.critical: CPA above break-even (you lose money per conversion).
recommendations: Diagnose whether the leak is front-end (CPC/CPM) or back-end (CVR)
Confirm the campaign optimises for the true value event
Improve landing page and offer to lift CVR
Refresh creative if fatigue is inflating front-end cost
best_practices: Always pair CPA with a documented max-sustainable-CPA target
Use CPA, not ROAS, when there is no per-order revenue (e.g. lead gen)
common_mistakes: Judging CPA without knowing the break-even line
Comparing CPA across different objectives
notes_edge_cases: For Adlytic's messaging-first accounts, the operative CPA variant is cost-per-messaging-conversation (see cost_per_message).

ROAS.name: Return on Ad Spend
ROAS.definition: Revenue generated per unit of currency spent on advertising. Meta: business profit generated per currency unit spent.
ROAS.meaning: Standard efficiency benchmark. A ROAS of 4.0 means £4 returned per £1 spent. As a pure ratio it is currency-factor-invariant (numerator and denominator scale together).
ROAS.warning: ROAS trending below your break-even ROAS (= 1 / margin).
ROAS.critical: ROAS below 1.0 (spending more than revenue returned) sustained over the attribution window.
recommendations: Compute break-even ROAS from margin and compare honestly
Lift AOV via bundles/upsells to raise ROAS without cutting cost
Address whichever of CPC/CPM/CVR is dragging CPA
Cross-check with blended MER to catch attribution gaps
best_practices: Track ROAS trend, not a single day
Validate platform ROAS against blended MER and true margin
common_mistakes: Treating platform-reported ROAS as ground truth despite attribution loss
Ignoring margin — a 3x ROAS can be unprofitable at thin margins
notes_edge_cases: ROAS is dimensionless: in Adlytic it is computed as revenueMinor/spendMinor and the currency factor cancels — it is never re-scaled during currency repair.
View-through-attributed ROAS overstates incremental value; prefer click-based or incrementality-tested ROAS for decisions.

frequency.name: Frequency
frequency.definition: The average number of times each unique person saw the ad within the reporting window. Motion: Impressions / Reach.
frequency.meaning: Saturation and fatigue indicator. Tolerance varies by audience temperature: cold audiences fatigue fast; retargeting tolerates more.
frequency.warning: Frequency above 3-4 on a cold audience, especially with CTR softening.
frequency.critical: Frequency climbing while CTR drops and CPA rises (classic fatigue signature).
possible_causes: Audience too small for the budget
recommendations: Rotate in fresh creative
Expand the audience or add lookalikes
Cap frequency or move budget to new segments
Exclude recent converters/engagers to reduce overexposure
best_practices: Read frequency together with CTR trend, not alone
Set audience-type-specific frequency ceilings
common_mistakes: Using one global frequency cap across cold and retargeting
Letting frequency climb unchecked on a small audience
notes_edge_cases: High frequency is acceptable and often efficient in retargeting where repetition drives conversion.

reach.name: Reach
reach.definition: The number of unique individuals who saw the ad at least once. Distinct from impressions, which counts every view including repeats.
reach.formula: count(unique users who saw the ad >= 1 time)
reach.meaning: Size of the unique audience touched. Combined with impressions it yields frequency.

benchmark.note: No universal benchmark; evaluate relative to audience size and objective (awareness favours reach; conversion favours depth).

reach.warning: Reach plateauing while spend and impressions keep rising (frequency inflating).
reach.critical: Reach stalled at a small fraction of addressable audience while frequency >4.
recommendations: Broaden targeting or add lookalikes
Increase audience size before increasing budget
Reduce over-restrictive exclusions
best_practices: Pair reach with frequency to judge saturation
For awareness objectives optimise reach efficiency (CPM per unique)
common_mistakes: Confusing reach with impressions
Scaling budget on a fixed small audience, inflating frequency
notes_edge_cases: Reach is deduplicated within a window but a person counted in two windows is not deduplicated across them.

impressions.name: Impressions
impressions.definition: The total number of times the ad was displayed on screen. Meta: counted when the ad instance first appears. One person can generate many impressions.
impressions.meaning: Raw exposure volume. The denominator for CTR and the numerator (with reach) for frequency.

benchmark.note: Volume metric; no quality benchmark in isolation.

impressions.warning: Impressions collapsing suddenly (delivery throttled, budget exhausted, or audience exhausted).
impressions.critical: Impressions near zero on an active, funded ad set (delivery failure or rejection).
possible_causes: Bid too low for the auction
recommendations: Check delivery status and policy review
Raise budget or bid if under-delivering
Broaden audience if exhausted
Resolve Learning Limited by consolidating or funding the ad set
best_practices: Use impressions only as a base for rate metrics, never as a success measure
common_mistakes: Treating high impressions as performance
Confusing impressions with reach
notes_edge_cases: An impression is counted on render, not on viewability; viewable-impression standards differ.

CVR.name: Conversion Rate
CVR.definition: The percentage of clicks (or link clicks) that resulted in the desired conversion. Good Morning: percentage of link clicks that converted.
CVR.meaning: Health of the post-click experience: landing page, offer, pricing, checkout, and availability. The bridge between traffic and revenue.
CVR.warning: CVR below 1.5% with healthy CTR (problem is post-click, not creative).
CVR.critical: CVR below 1% on a conversion campaign with adequate traffic.
possible_causes: Offer/price mismatch with the ad's promise
Checkout friction or required account creation
recommendations: Audit landing page speed and mobile UX
Align landing page message to the ad (scent matching)
Reduce checkout steps; offer guest checkout
Verify inventory and pricing
Tighten audience intent
best_practices: Diagnose with the CTR+CVR matrix: stable CTR + falling CVR = post-click issue
Test landing pages independently of creative
common_mistakes: Blaming creative when the leak is post-click
Changing many variables at once, losing the signal
notes_edge_cases: CVR denominator matters — clicks vs link clicks vs landing-page-views give very different rates; state which you use.

cost_per_message.name: Cost Per Messaging Conversation Started
cost_per_message.definition: The average spend to start one new messaging conversation (Messenger / Instagram / WhatsApp) via a click-to-message campaign. Meta counts a conversation as started on the first message in a 7-day window.
cost_per_message.meaning: Adlytic's Phase-1 primary KPI. The acquisition cost of a live sales conversation rather than a direct purchase. Must be read against close-rate and order value to judge true profitability.

benchmark.note: Region- and vertical-dependent; growing objective with sparse public benchmarks. Establish a per-account baseline and compare against it (Adlytic uses relative baselining).

cost_per_message.warning: Cost per conversation rising >25% above the account baseline (see Adlytic CostPerMessageEngine).
cost_per_message.critical: Cost per conversation above the value of a conversation (spend exceeds expected margin per closed chat).
possible_causes: Weak creative hook reducing click-to-message rate
Friction in the message template / greeting
recommendations: Test message-led creative and a clear 'message us' CTA
Optimise the automated greeting/qualifying questions
Target audiences with conversational intent
Refresh creative when frequency climbs
Reduce CPM via broader placements
best_practices: Count exactly one canonical 'conversation started' event — do not sum 'started' + 'first reply' (double-counts)
Pair with close-rate to derive true cost per sale
common_mistakes: Summing overlapping messaging action types and inflating the count ~1.9x
Judging cost per message without the downstream close rate
notes_edge_cases: Adlytic's insightMapper intentionally picks ONE messaging action_type (conversation_started_7d) and never sums, to match Meta Ads Manager's reported count.
